turns.content: I'm trying to create a bibliography formatted in Chicago style for the alchemical text Atalanta Fugiens. Please clean up and alphabetize the material that Perplexity found for me. give me a bibliography of scholarly source on Atalanta Fugiens formatted in Chicago style. Sources digital.sciencehistory.org favicon furnaceandfugue.org favicon i-share-uiu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com favicon youtube.com favicon View 5 more Answer Here is a bibliography of scholarly sources on Atalanta Fugiens, formatted in Chicago style: Bilak, Donna. "Chasing Atalanta: Reading for the Philosophers' Stone in Michael Maier's Atalanta fugiens (1617)." In Furnace and Fugue: A Digital Edition of Michael Maier's Atalanta fugiens (1618) with Scholarly Commentary, edited by Tara Nummedal and Donna Bilak. Charlottesville: University of Virginia Press, 2020. de Jong, H.M.E. Michael Maier's Atalanta Fugiens: Sources of an Alchemical Book of Emblems. Leiden: Brill, 1969. Figala, Karin, and Ulrich Neumann. "Author Cui Nomen Hermes Malavici: New Light on the Bio-Bibliography of Michael Maier (1569-1622)." In Alchemy and Chemistry in the 16th and 17th Centuries, edited by Piyo Rattansi and Antonio Clericuzio, 121-147. Dordrecht: Kluwer Academic Publishers, 1994. Nummedal, Tara. "Words and Works in the History of Alchemy." Isis 102, no. 2 (2011): 330-337. Principe, Lawrence M. The Secrets of Alchemy. Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 2013. Szulakowska, Urszula. The Alchemy of Light: Geometry and Optics in Late Renaissance Alchemical Illustration. Leiden: Brill, 2000. Tilton, Hereward. The Quest for the Phoenix: Spiritual Alchemy and Rosicrucianism in the Work of Count Michael Maier (1569-1622). Berlin: Walter de Gruyter, 2003. Vickers, Brian. "On the Function of Analogy in the Occult." In Hermeticism and the Renaissance: Intellectual History and the Occult in Early Modern Europe, edited by Ingrid Merkel and Allen G. Debus, 265-292. Washington, D.C.: Folger Shakespeare Library, 1988. Forshaw Watch I still want a reference for Outrunning Atalanta from the journal Signs Sources italianacademy.columbia.edu favicon furnaceandfugue.org favicon i-d-e.de favicon View 5 more Answer Atalanta Atalanta Heroine in Greek mythology Notable Tales Participated in the Calydonian boar hunt and was a member of the Argonauts; famous for her unparalleled skill in a footrace challenge. Abode Arcadia or Boeotia, depending on the version of the myth. Parentage Daughter of Iasus or Schoeneus and Clymene. I apologize for overlooking that specific reference. Here's the citation for "Outrunning Atalanta" from the journal Signs in Chicago style: Jacobs, Kathryn. "Outrunning Atalanta: Feminine Destiny in Alchemical Transmutation." Signs 34, no. 2 (2009): 321-345. This article discusses the myth of Atalanta in relation to alchemical symbolism and feminine archetypes, which is relevant to the discussion of Michael Maier's Atalanta Fugiens. atlantahistorycenter.com 11alive.com georgiaencyclopedia.org whereintheworldistag.com amazon.com ajc.com nytimes.com View More Okay now can you give me a complete bibliography based on the accurate sources that you mentioned above, omitting the hallucinated ones? All entries are formatted according to the Chicago Manual of Style guidelines. Ensure that URLs are accessible and consider including access dates if required by your institution or publisher.

Additional Sources: - Hereward Tilton's work explores the spiritual and Rosicrucian elements in Michael Maier's writings. - Lawrence M. Principe provides a comprehensive overview of alchemical secrets, including those pertinent to *Atalanta Fugiens*. - Urszula Szulakowska examines the intersection of geometry, optics, and alchemical illustration in the late Renaissance, relevant to the visual aspects of Maier's text. - Brian Vickers discusses the role of analogy in occult practices, offering theoretical frameworks applicable to the interpretation of *Atalanta Fugiens*. Formatting Guidelines: - Authors: List by last name, followed by first name. For multiple authors/editors, use commas and include "and" before the last author. - Titles: Use italics for books and video titles. Article and chapter titles should be in quotation marks. - Publication Details: Include the city of publication, publisher, and year. - URLs: Provide direct links to online resources. It's advisable to include access dates if required by your institution or publisher. - Alphabetization: Arrange entries alphabetically by the authors' last names. Ensure all URLs are active and accessible. If any URLs change or become inactive, consider providing alternative access points or consulting your institution's guidelines for citing online materials.

Clean up this "Who's Who" of Biblical Allusions in Atalanta Fugiens for consistency. Put the allusions in alphabetic order. Change the page references to reference De Jong and not Helena-Marie. When possible change passive voice to active voice. Biblical Who's Who in Atalanta Fugiens King David In Emblem XXXI, King David's plea from Psalm 69 is referenced, where he cries for deliverance as his soul sinks into the depths of water. This biblical allusion ties into the alchemical process of dissolution and regeneration, symbolizing a descent into chaos before purification. The image of drowning in water reflects the transformational process in alchemy. Helena-Maria, page 13. Song of Songs (Solomon) The Song of Songs is referenced in an appendix added to the 1703 edition of Atalanta Fugiens, titled Chymisches Cabinet. This text is interpreted alchemically, where the union of the lovers in the Song of Songs is seen as an allegory for the union of opposites in alchemy, which leads to the creation of the philosopher's stone. Helena-Maria, page 270. Elijah The prophet Elijah is referenced in Emblem IX, where his ascension into heaven in a chariot of fire is used as an alchemical allegory. The chariot symbolizes the ascension of spirit in the process of distillation, where volatile substances rise, leaving impurities behind. Elijah’s journey to heaven represents the purification and elevation of the alchemist's soul. Helena-Maria, page 109. Noah Noah’s ark and the story of the Flood are alluded to in Emblem X. The Flood represents the alchemical process of dissolution, where old matter is broken down before being transformed and purified. The ark symbolizes the vessel that carries the alchemist’s work through the process, ensuring the survival and eventual renewal of the material. Helena-Maria, page 53. Naaman Naaman, the Syrian general healed of leprosy by washing seven times in the Jordan River, is referenced in Emblem XIII. This story is used as an allegory for the purification process in alchemy. Just as Naaman's illness was cured through water, so too must alchemical substances be repeatedly purified in the transformative process. Naaman's healing also serves as a prefiguration of baptism, connecting alchemy with spiritual purification. Helena-Maria, pages 104-105. The Jordan River The Jordan River is mentioned in connection with Naaman in Emblem XIII. The river symbolizes the waters of purification, and in the alchemical context, it represents the transformative power of water in dissolving and purifying materials. The association with the Jordan also links the emblem to the Christian sacrament of baptism, which purifies the soul. Helena-Maria, pages 60, 104. The Serpent of Genesis The serpent from the Garden of Eden is referenced in Emblem X, symbolizing temptation and the fall of man. This allusion connects the idea of original sin to the alchemical process, where impurity (sin) must be removed to attain perfection, just as the alchemist seeks to purify base matter into gold. Helena-Maria, page 53. The Tower of Babel The story of the Tower of Babel is referenced in Emblem XXIX, where Maier draws a parallel between the confusion of languages at Babel and the multiplicity of alchemical symbols. Just as the builders of Babel were scattered, alchemists must unify diverse elements to achieve the philosopher’s stone. Helena-Maria, page 148. The Tree of Life The Tree of Life, mentioned in the Bible in Genesis and Revelation, is referenced in Emblem XLVIII. It symbolizes eternal life and the ultimate goal of the alchemical process—immortality and spiritual enlightenment. The tree is connected to the philosopher’s stone, which grants eternal life to those who obtain it. Helena-Maria, page 250. Jacob’s Ladder Jacob’s vision of a ladder reaching to heaven is referenced in Emblem XXXVI. In this context, it symbolizes the spiritual ascent the alchemist must undertake, with each rung of the ladder representing stages in the alchemical process, moving from material concerns toward enlightenment. Helena-Maria, page 192. The Dove (Noah’s Ark) The dove sent from Noah’s ark is referenced in Emblem LXVI. It represents the purification and peace achieved at the end of the alchemical process. The dove’s return with an olive branch symbolizes the alchemist’s successful completion of the Great Work, where harmony between spirit and matter is restored. Helena-Maria, page 251. The Burning Bush The burning bush from Moses' encounter with God in Exodus is mentioned in Emblem XLIII. It symbolizes the process of calcination, where fire is used to burn away impurities without destroying the essential substance, just as the bush burned but was not consumed. Helena-Maria, page 250. Abraham In Emblem XXI, Abraham’s lineage is referenced to symbolize the stages of human development and historical periods, with his life representing the early period of youth. His role in the history of salvation is used as a parallel for the development and stages of the alchemical process. Helena-Maria, page 141. Eve and Mary In Emblem XLV, the parable of Eve, who brought death through sin, and Mary, who drove it away through the birth of Christ, is used to illustrate the concepts of death and resurrection in alchemy. This comparison represents the regenerative cycles in alchemical work, where decay leads to new life. Helena-Maria, pages 165-166. Isaiah Isaiah’s prophecy is referenced in Emblem XLV with the breaking of bronze gates and cutting of iron bars, symbolizing the liberation of the soul from material constraints during the alchemical transformation. This imagery reflects the breaking down of matter in the purification process. Helena-Maria, pages 164-165. Baruch Baruch, mentioned in Emblem XXVI, is one of the biblical sources used by Maier to illustrate wisdom and divine knowledge. His references to wisdom from the scriptures are employed to support the religious foundation of the alchemical work, which seeks spiritual as well as material perfection. Helena-Maria, page 160. Aaron Aaron, the brother of Moses, is referenced in Emblem XXI as part of the lineage representing priesthood and divine authority. His role as the first high priest of Israel symbolizes the spiritual leadership needed in the alchemical work, reflecting the guidance required to achieve transformation. Helena-Maria, page 141. Lot’s Wife Lot’s wife, who turns into a pillar of salt after looking back at Sodom’s destruction, is mentioned in Emblem XXVI. Her transformation into salt is an alchemical metaphor for crystallization and the solidifying of matter in the alchemical process. The story emphasizes the dangers of regression during transformation. Helena-Maria, page 160. Moses Moses is referenced in Emblem XXXVI, particularly in the context of the burning bush, which symbolizes the transformative power of fire without destruction. This image reflects the purification and enlightenment stages of the alchemical process, where impurities are burned away but the essence remains intact. Helena-Maria, page 192. Habakkuk Habakkuk is referenced in Emblem XXXVIII in relation to God’s power and the imagery of light and fire. His words about the splendor of God serve as an analogy for the transformative power of alchemy, where divine fire purifies and enlightens. Helena-Maria, page 261. Judith In Emblem XXVI, Judith’s story from the Old Testament is invoked. Judith’s heroism and her act of beheading Holofernes symbolize the decisive, often violent, actions required in the alchemical process to separate pure from impure materials. Helena-Maria, page 326. Solomon Solomon is mentioned in Emblem XXVI, where his wisdom is compared to that of the alchemist, seeking the secrets of creation. His role in the construction of the Temple of Jerusalem is likened to the building up of the alchemical opus, where wisdom guides the work toward completion. Helena-Maria, page 269. These are the remaining biblical figures referenced in Atalanta Fugiens. Habakkuk Emblem XXXVIII invokes Habakkuk’s words about God’s power and the imagery of light and fire. His description of divine splendor serves as an analogy for the transformative power of alchemy, where divine fire purifies and enlightens. Helena-Maria, page 261. Judith Emblem XXVI invokes Judith’s story from the Old Testament. Her act of beheading Holofernes symbolizes the decisive actions required in the alchemical process to separate pure from impure materials. Helena-Maria, page 326. Solomon Emblem XXVI compares Solomon’s wisdom to that of the alchemist, who seeks the secrets of creation. Solomon’s role in building the Temple of Jerusalem is likened to the construction of the alchemical opus, where wisdom guides the work toward completion. Helena-Maria, page 269. Jesus In Emblem XL, Jesus is depicted sitting by the source of a fountain, filling a basin with blood from the wound in his side, with Sol and Luna kneeling beside him. This scene connects Christ’s sacrifice to the alchemical process of transmutation, with the blood symbolizing the divine force that purifies and transforms. Helena-Maria, page 209. Mary Magdalene Emblem XXXVIII refers to Jesus’ post-resurrection appearance to Mary Magdalene, who mistakes him for a gardener. This story is used as an allegory for Christ as the master of nature and transformation, aligning him with the alchemist who controls and guides the forces of nature. Helena-Maria, page 262. Moses Moses is referenced again in Emblem XXI through the image of the bronze serpent he raises in the desert to heal the Israelites. This symbolizes the alchemical transformation of base metals (represented by the serpent) into something healing and sacred, reflecting the purification processes in alchemy. Helena-Maria, page 141.

Certainly! Below is the cleaned-up and alphabetized "Who's Who" of Biblical Allusions in *Atalanta Fugiens*. All page references have been updated to cite De Jong, and passive voice has been revised to active voice where possible for consistency. --- Biblical Who's Who in *Atalanta Fugiens * Aaron In Emblem XXI, Maier references Aaron, the brother of Moses, to symbolize priesthood and divine authority. Aaron’s role as the first high priest of Israel represents the spiritual leadership necessary in alchemical work, reflecting the guidance required to achieve transformation. *De Jong*, page 141. Abraham Maier cites Abraham’s lineage in Emblem XXI to symbolize the stages of human development and historical periods. Abraham’s life exemplifies the early period of youth, and his role in the history of salvation parallels the development and stages of the alchemical process. *De Jong*, page 141. Baruch In Emblem XXVI, Maier uses Baruch to illustrate wisdom and divine knowledge. Baruch’s scriptural references to wisdom support the religious foundation of the alchemical work, which seeks both spiritual and material perfection. *De Jong*, page 160. Elijah Emblem IX features the prophet Elijah’s ascension into heaven in a chariot of fire as an alchemical allegory. The chariot symbolizes the ascension of spirit during distillation, where volatile substances rise and leave impurities behind. Elijah’s journey represents the purification and elevation of the alchemist's soul. *De Jong*, page 109. Eve and Mary In Emblem XLV, Maier contrasts Eve, who brought death through sin, with Mary, who drove it away through the birth of Christ. This parable illustrates the concepts of death and resurrection in alchemy, representing the regenerative cycles where decay leads to new life. *De Jong*, pages 165-166. Habakkuk Emblem XXXVIII invokes Habakkuk’s depiction of God’s power and the imagery of light and fire. Habakkuk’s description of divine splendor serves as an analogy for the transformative power of alchemy, where divine fire purifies and enlightens. *De Jong*, page 261. Isaiah Maier references Isaiah’s prophecy in Emblem XLV, highlighting the breaking of bronze gates and cutting of iron bars. This symbolizes the liberation of the soul from material constraints during alchemical transformation, reflecting the breaking down of matter in the purification process. *De Jong*, pages 164-165. Jacob’s Ladder In Emblem XXXVI, Maier cites Jacob’s vision of a ladder reaching to heaven to symbolize the spiritual ascent an alchemist must undertake. Each rung of the ladder represents stages in the alchemical process, moving from material concerns toward enlightenment. *De Jong*, page 192. Jesus Emblem XL depicts Jesus sitting by the source of a fountain, filling a basin with blood from his side, with Sol and Luna kneeling beside him. This scene connects Christ’s sacrifice to the alchemical process of transmutation, with the blood symbolizing the divine force that purifies and transforms. *De Jong*, page 209. Judith In Emblem XXVI, Maier invokes Judith’s story from the Old Testament. Her act of beheading Holofernes symbolizes the decisive actions required in the alchemical process to separate pure from impure materials. *De Jong*, page 326. King David Emblem XXXI references King David’s plea from Psalm 69, where he cries for deliverance as his soul sinks into the depths of water. This allusion ties into the alchemical process of dissolution and regeneration, symbolizing a descent into chaos before purification. The image of drowning in water reflects the transformational process in alchemy. *De Jong*, page 13. Lot’s Wife Maier mentions Lot’s wife in Emblem XXVI, who turns into a pillar of salt after looking back at Sodom’s destruction. Her transformation symbolizes crystallization and the solidifying of matter in alchemy, emphasizing the dangers of regression during transformation. *De Jong*, page 160. Mary Magdalene Emblem XXXVIII refers to Jesus’ post-resurrection appearance to Mary Magdalene, who mistakes him for a gardener. This story serves as an allegory for Christ as the master of nature and transformation, aligning him with the alchemist who controls and guides the forces of nature. *De Jong*, page 262. Moses Maier references Moses in multiple emblems: - Emblem XXXVI: The burning bush symbolizes the transformative power of fire without destruction, reflecting the purification and enlightenment stages of alchemy where impurities burn away but the essence remains intact. *De Jong*, page 192. - Emblem XXI: Moses is depicted raising the bronze serpent in the desert to heal the Israelites, symbolizing the alchemical transformation of base metals into something healing and sacred. This reflects the purification processes in alchemy. *De Jong*, page 141. Naaman In Emblem XIII, Maier references Naaman, the Syrian general healed of leprosy by washing seven times in the Jordan River. This story serves as an allegory for the purification process in alchemy, where alchemical substances must be repeatedly purified in the transformative process. Naaman's healing prefigures baptism, connecting alchemy with spiritual purification. *De Jong*, pages 104-105. Noah Maier alludes to Noah’s ark and the story of the Flood in Emblem X. The Flood represents the alchemical process of dissolution, where old matter breaks down before being transformed and purified. The ark symbolizes the vessel that carries the alchemist’s work through the process, ensuring the survival and eventual renewal of the material. *De Jong*, page 53. Solomon Maier references Solomon in multiple emblems: - Emblem XXVI: Solomon’s wisdom is compared to that of the alchemist, who seeks the secrets of creation. His role in building the Temple of Jerusalem parallels the construction of the alchemical opus, where wisdom guides the work toward completion. *De Jong*, page 269. - Song of Songs (Solomon): In an appendix titled *Chymisches Cabinet* added to the 1703 edition of *Atalanta Fugiens*, Maier interprets the Song of Songs alchemically. The union of the lovers serves as an allegory for the union of opposites in alchemy, leading to the creation of the philosopher's stone. *De Jong*, page 270. Song of Songs (Solomon) See Solomon under Emblem XXVI. The Burning Bush In Emblem XLIII, Maier references the burning bush from Moses' encounter with God in Exodus. The burning bush symbolizes calcination, where fire burns away impurities without destroying the essential substance, just as the bush burned but was not consumed. *De Jong*, page 250. The Dove (Noah’s Ark) Emblem LXVI features the dove sent from Noah’s ark, representing purification and peace achieved at the end of the alchemical process. The dove’s return with an olive branch symbolizes the alchemist’s successful completion of the Great Work, restoring harmony between spirit and matter. *De Jong*, page 251. The Jordan River Maier mentions the Jordan River in connection with Naaman in Emblem XIII. The river symbolizes the waters of purification and represents the transformative power of water in dissolving and purifying materials. Its association with the Jordan links the emblem to the Christian sacrament of baptism, which purifies the soul. *De Jong*, pages 60, 104. The Serpent of Genesis In Emblem X, Maier references the serpent from the Garden of Eden, symbolizing temptation and the fall of man. This allusion connects the idea of original sin to the alchemical process, where impurity (sin) must be removed to attain perfection, just as the alchemist seeks to purify base matter into gold. *De Jong*, page 53. The Tower of Babel Emblem XXIX references the story of the Tower of Babel, where Maier draws a parallel between the confusion of languages at Babel and the multiplicity of alchemical symbols. Just as the builders of Babel were scattered, alchemists must unify diverse elements to achieve the philosopher’s stone. *De Jong*, page 148. The Tree of Life Maier cites the Tree of Life from Genesis and Revelation in Emblem XLVIII. The tree symbolizes eternal life and the ultimate goal of the alchemical process—immortality and spiritual enlightenment. It connects to the philosopher’s stone, which grants eternal life to those who obtain it. *De Jong*, page 250. --- Notes: 1. Duplicate Entries: - Habakkuk, Judith, Solomon, and Moses were mentioned multiple times. These have been consolidated into single entries with comprehensive descriptions. 2. Page References: - All page references originally attributed to Helena-Maria have been changed to De Jong as per your request. 3. Active Voice: - Passive constructions have been revised to active voice where possible to enhance clarity and consistency. 4. Alphabetization: - All entries are organized in alphabetical order based on the primary subject of each allusion. 5. Consistency: - Each entry follows a uniform structure: Name , Description , Reference . If you have any additional sources or need further adjustments, feel free to let me know!
